goal: Design the authentication boundary for the new admin console
non_goals: Redesigning the end-user authentication flow (separate product surface)
Implementing fine-grained RBAC beyond the initial admin/super-admin split
hard_constraints: Must integrate with the company's existing Okta tenant (no replacing the IdP)
Admin console must be inaccessible without authentication — no anonymous read paths

branches.title: Ingress-Layer Sidecar (OAuth2 Proxy)
branches.thesis: Enforce authentication at the ingress layer using an OAuth2 Proxy sidecar, so the admin console backend never sees unauthenticated requests.
branches.proposed_design:
Deploy `oauth2-proxy` as a sidecar on the ingress controller.

- oauth2-proxy container in the ingress pod
- Kubernetes Ingress resource with auth-url annotation
- A Kubernetes Secret holding the Okta client credentials
branches.why_this_wins: The authentication concern is fully pushed to infrastructure.
branches.tradeoffs: The sidecar adds latency per request for the auth check.
branches.failure_modes: If the Okta JWKS endpoint is unreachable and the proxy's cache is cold, authentication may fail.
branches.verification_plan: Deploy the sidecar in a staging namespace and confirm unauthenticated requests return 302.
branches.first_implementation_slice: Stand up oauth2-proxy in the staging namespace with Okta OIDC config.
branches.rejects_from_others: Branch 2 argues a dedicated gateway is more future-proof. Rejected for a single-service app.

branches.title: Dedicated API Gateway with JWT Verification
branches.thesis: Route all admin console traffic through a dedicated API Gateway that verifies Okta-issued JWTs.
branches.proposed_design:
Deploy Kong in front of the admin console.

- Gateway instance with a JWT plugin configured against Okta's JWKS endpoint
- A Consumer in Kong representing the admin console
- A gateway-level rate limiter and IP allowlist
branches.why_this_wins: The gateway becomes the single auth enforcement point for all internal tools.
branches.tradeoffs: The gateway is a new piece of infrastructure that the team must operate and monitor.
branches.failure_modes: Gateway misconfiguration silently blocks all admin access with no fallback path.

operational_complexity.justification: Kong adds a stateful, high-availability infrastructure component.

branches.verification_plan: Issue a valid Okta JWT and confirm the gateway proxies the request to the admin console.
branches.first_implementation_slice: Deploy Kong in DB-less mode in the staging cluster with a single JWT plugin.
branches.rejects_from_others: Branch 1 is simpler for a single service but does not generalize to future internal tools.

branches.title: Application-Layer Middleware with Okta SDK
branches.thesis: Implement authentication directly in the SvelteKit application layer using the Okta Node.js SDK and server hooks.
branches.proposed_design:
Add the @okta/okta-auth-js library to the admin console's dependencies.

- src/hooks.server.ts with the handle function
- An Okta OIDC callback route at /auth/callback
- A src/lib/server/auth.ts module with requireAuth and requireSuperAdmin helpers
branches.why_this_wins: Developers have full visibility into and control over the auth flow.
branches.tradeoffs: Authentication is now a first-class application concern that must be maintained and audited.
branches.failure_modes: A developer adds a new route group and forgets the auth layout guard — the route is publicly accessible.

operational_complexity.justification: Auth logic lives in application code; no additional infra beyond the existing Postgres instance.

branches.verification_plan: Run the SvelteKit dev server with a real Okta OIDC flow and confirm login redirects.
branches.first_implementation_slice: Implement the handle hook in hooks.server.ts with a mock user injection for dev mode.
branches.rejects_from_others: Branches 1 and 2 push auth to infrastructure, which this branch rejects in favor of developer control.

cells.characterization: Single well-understood sidecar; Redis adds minor overhead
Gateway is powerful but adds significant ops surface area
Auth in app code is familiar but requires ongoing discipline
App never sees unauthenticated traffic; smallest custom surface
Gateway is well-audited but adds configuration risk
Custom auth code is highest-risk; one missed guard exposes a route

branches_agree: All three branches agree that Okta is the identity provider and that OIDC is the correct protocol.
branches_disagree: The core disagreement is about where authentication responsibility should live: at the infrastructure boundary or inside the application.
hidden_assumptions: All branches assume the Okta tenant is reliably available during authentication flows. Branch 2 assumes **future internal tools** will be built and benefit from the gateway investment. Branch 3 assumes `@okta/okta-auth-js` is stable and long-term-supported.

recommendation.rationale: Branch 1 (Ingress-Layer Sidecar) is the right choice for the current context. The sidecar approach achieves the required security guarantee with the smallest infrastructure change.

uncertainty: The primary uncertainty is whether the current Kubernetes ingress controller supports the nginx auth-url annotation pattern.
next_action: Confirm the Kubernetes ingress controller type and version with the platform team.
